summary:
Dimini is a Neolithic site near Volos in Thessaly, central Greece, best known for the later Dimini culture (c. 4800–4500 BCE) that succeeded the [[event:1416|Sesklo culture]] in the same region. The proto-phase of occupation at the site during the period covered here represents the earlier Sesklo-period occupation of the location, prior to the development of the distinctive Dimini culture with its concentric stone enclosures and elaborate painted pottery.

The site of Dimini's location in the fertile Thessalian plain made it an attractive settlement location throughout the Neolithic, and the site's long occupation sequence provides a rare continuous record of cultural development across the entire Greek Neolithic. The Sesklo-period occupation at the site, part of the earliest Neolithic farming horizon in Greece, reflects the established agricultural economy of emmer wheat, lentils, and herded sheep, goats, and cattle that characterized early Greek Neolithic communities.

The Dimini site is today a protected archaeological park and important heritage site, and its well-known concentric stone walls of the later (mature) Dimini phase have generated considerable debate about whether they represent defensive fortifications, stock enclosures, or social boundary markers — reflecting the increased social complexity of the later Greek Neolithic.
